title: Symplectic homology via Gromov-Witten theory
speaker: Luis Diogo
abstract: Symplectic homology is a very useful tool in symplectic topology, but it can be hard to compute explicitly. We will describe a procedure for computing symplectic homology using counts of pseudo-holomorphic spheres. These counts can sometimes be performed using Gromov-Witten theory. This method is applicable to a class of manifolds that are obtained by removing, from a closed symplectic manifold, a symplectic hypersurface of codimension 2. This is joint work with Samuel Lisi.

proof_stages.label: Divisor-complement Floer setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets the goal of computing symplectic homology for the completion W of the complement of a monotone symplectic divisor Sigma in a closed monotone symplectic manifold X, with contact boundary Y an S1-bundle over Sigma whose Reeb flow runs along the fibers.

proof_stages.label: Morse-Bott Hamiltonian generators
proof_stages.summary: For radial Hamiltonians H=h(e^r) growing on the cylindrical end, the one-periodic orbits split into constant orbits and Morse-Bott families of Reeb-fiber orbits, and auxiliary Morse functions on W, Sigma, and critical S1 fibers give the Floer chain generators q-check and q-hat with multiplicity k.

proof_stages.label: Neck-stretched split Floer differential
proof_stages.summary: Stretching the neck along the contact hypersurface Y degenerates relevant Floer cylinders into punctured Floer cylinders in the symplectization R x Y together with possible holomorphic planes in W, thereby isolating the part of the differential where the S1-symmetry can be exploited.

proof_stages.label: S1-symmetric ansatz for symplectization cylinders
proof_stages.summary: With an S1-invariant almost complex structure, every relevant Floer cylinder in R x Y is shown to arise uniquely, modulo R x S1 ambiguity, from a J-holomorphic cylinder plus a solution (f1,f2) of an explicit auxiliary PDE, and in index one the auxiliary solution is essentially unique.

proof_stages.label: Projection to Gromov-Witten curve counts
proof_stages.summary: Projecting finite-energy J-holomorphic cylinders in R x Y to Sigma yields removable singularities and hence holomorphic spheres in Sigma, while lost fiber information is recovered by meromorphic sections and the W-planes correspond to relative holomorphic spheres in X, so the full Floer differential can be reconstructed from Gromov-Witten-type sphere counts.

examples.name: Cotangent-bundle symplectic homology computation
examples.note: The known identification of symplectic homology of a cotangent bundle with the homology of the free loop space motivates the desire for comparable computations beyond cotangent bundles.
examples.engagement_reason: The cotangent-bundle computation is cited as a known result, SH(T*B) equals free-loop-space homology, and later used only as a comparison answer for T*S2 without carrying out the computation.

passes.reason: Cotangent bundles form a standard infinite class and the known SH computation is used as a comparison, not to refute a claim.
A cotangent-bundle computation is a standard infinite-family benchmark for symplectic homology, used for comparison rather than refutation.

examples.name: Donaldson symplectic hypersurfaces
examples.note: Donaldson's theorem is cited as a broad source of divisors satisfying the Poincare-duality condition in the setup, illustrating that the hypotheses are not vacuous.
examples.engagement_reason: Donaldson hypersurfaces are invoked as a source of examples satisfying the setup, with no construction or calculation performed on a particular hypersurface.

passes.reason: Donaldson hypersurfaces occur systematically for large multiples of an integral symplectic class and are cited as a source of examples.

examples.name: Projective varieties with ample divisors
examples.note: Projective varieties with ample divisors are presented as a particularly useful class of inputs where Gromov-Witten tools can be imported for the eventual curve counts.
examples.engagement_reason: Projective varieties with ample divisors are described as an important class where the assumptions often hold and Gromov-Witten tools apply, but no specific case is worked through.

examples.name: CP1 times CP1 with diagonal divisor
examples.note: The running model X=CP1 x CP1 with Sigma the diagonal, giving Y=RP3 and W comparable to T*S2, makes the abstract generators and the final loop-space comparison concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses CP1×CP1 with diagonal CP1 as a running model, identifies the contact boundary and complement, chooses Morse functions, and explicitly lists the resulting Floer chain generators.

passes.reason: The pair CP1×CP1 with the diagonal divisor is a homogeneous highly regular running model, not an obstruction or sporadic exception.
CP1 times CP1 with the diagonal is a highly homogeneous running model whose complement is T*S2, used illustratively rather than as a counterexample.
